Fresh cheeses like ricotta, mozzarella, or feta (when young) retain more of the milk’s original lactose because they’re made quickly and often not aged for more than a few days or weeks. In contrast, aged Mediterranean cheeses — think Parmigiano-Reggiano, manchego curado, or a well-aged pecorino — can spend months or even years maturing. During that time, the bacteria used in cheese fermentation continue to break down lactose into lactic acid. Extensive drainage of whey (which contains most of the lactose) during production also helps reduce residual sugar. By the time the cheese is ready to eat, the lactose level often drops to trace amounts, sometimes so low they’re undetectable in lab tests.

Another factor is enzymatic activity: the cultures used in traditional and artisanal cheesemaking produce enzymes that not only develop flavor but also aid in pre-digesting some milk proteins, giving these cheeses a richer, more complex taste profile that can feel less taxing to the gut.

In Mediterranean cooking, this means you can often enjoy small amounts of aged, hard cheeses without the same reaction you might get from milk or soft cheeses. But it’s still a good idea to test your tolerance gradually, because levels can vary slightly depending on the producer and the aging method.

So — it’s a mix of microbiology, time, and tradition: the aging process essentially converts a potentially tricky ingredient into something your body may accept more readily, all while enhancing its flavor complexity.

With **long-aged cheeses** (think Parmigiano-Reggiano, Manchego curado, or aged cheddar), the lactose content is typically negligible because the ripening phase gives bacteria more time to consume what little lactose remains after curd separation. Aging also allows enzymatic and microbial activity to keep breaking down residual sugars, leaving the cheese almost lactose-free by the time it’s ready to eat. This often means such cheeses are better tolerated by those who are otherwise sensitive to dairy sugars.

**Fresh cheeses** (mozzarella, ricotta, burrata, young feta) undergo little to no aging, so they retain more of their original lactose. That means the body must rely more heavily on natural lactase production (in the small intestine) or an added lactase enzyme to break it down — processes that can be less efficient in some adults, leading to digestive discomfort.

When you introduce **wine pairing** into the mix, there’s a subtle physiological interaction: the acids and tannins in wine can slightly alter gastrointestinal transit time and the environment of the small intestine. High-acidity wines (like Sauvignon Blanc or some young Tempranillos) may modestly accelerate digestion, which could, in theory, amplify symptoms if residual lactose is present — though this effect is highly individual. Conversely, pairing aged, low-lactose cheeses with complementary wines (nutty Parmigiano with a dry sparkling, for instance) generally avoids such issues, delivering all the sensory pleasure without much risk of discomfort.

In Mediterranean dining traditions — which you probably know well given your culinary leanings — naturally aged, hard cheeses are often served in smaller portions at the end of a meal, not only for flavor balance but also because they’re gentler for those sensitive to milk sugars. This pairing practice evolved before the science was fully understood — yet it aligns remarkably well with what we now know about lactose chemistry and digestion.

Proteins, particularly the casein that remains dense in aged cheeses, also contribute to slower digestion and provide a buffering effect against the acidity of certain wines.

Interestingly, when a rich, fatty aged cheese is paired with a high-acid wine, the wine’s acidity can help cleanse the palate and balance mouthfeel, while the fat helps temper the perceived sharpness of the wine. It’s worth noting that these effects are subtle and vary from person to person. But from a culinary science standpoint, the interplay of low lactose, higher fat/protein, and mindful wine pairing can create a more enjoyable — and physiologically gentle — tasting experience.
